summary: A militaristic East Slavic people of the Eastern European steppe frontier, especially along the Don and Dnieper Rivers, organized into self-governing hosts led by elected chieftains called hetmans and atamans. Clues most often identify them through their leadership councils (rada), the Zaporozhian Sich founded on the island of Khortytsia, the Khmelnytsky Uprising against the Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth, and Ilya Repin's painting The Reply of the Zaporozhian Cossacks to Sultan Mehmed IV, in which they laughingly compose an insult-filled letter refusing an Ottoman sultan's demand for surrender. They also figure heavily in Russian literature (Tolstoy's The Cossacks, Sholokhov's And Quiet Flows the Don, Gogol's Taras Bulba), in revolts against the tsars (Razin, Bulavin, Pugachev), and in their fraught twentieth-century history of service against and defection from the Soviet Union in World War II followed by forced repatriation.

works.name: General / Giveaway Identifiers
works.description: A militaristic East Slavic people of the steppe frontier, many living along the Don River in present-day Ukraine and Russia, who served as frontier horsemen for the Polish and Russian armies in exchange for a degree of political autonomy. They governed themselves through community assemblies called rada, which elected chieftains known as hetmans and other officers called atamans, and their highest symbol of power was a gem-encrusted mace called the bulawa. A papal diplomat, Carlo Gamberini, reported that they never ate bread and that their name derived from a term showing the comparative agility of goats, while an 18th-century manuscript called the Eyewitness Chronicle records their early history. The British historian Shane O'Rourke has argued that certain twentieth-century ethnic-assimilation measures directed against them could constitute genocide.

Name these nomadic people who often fought for Russia in exchange for political autonomy, though under leaders like Yemelyan Pugachev they sometimes revolted against the Russian government.

clues.clue: Governed themselves through community councils called rada, which elected chieftains called hetmans and other officers called atamans
examples: "Hetman" and "ataman" were leadership titles used among what group of frontier horsemen who patrolled the steppes of imperial Russia?
These people organized themselves in councils called rada which elected leaders called atamans.

clues.clue: A papal diplomat, Carlo Gamberini, reported that they never ate bread and that their name derived from a term showing the comparative agility of goats
examples: The papal diplomat Carlo Gamberini reported that these people "never eat bread" and that they got their name from showing the comparative agility of goats.

clues.clue: Their highest symbol of power was a gem-encrusted mace called the bulawa
examples: The bulawa, a gem-encrusted mace, was the highest symbol of power for these people.

clues.clue: An 18th-century manuscript called the "Eyewitness Chronicle" records their history; their first major settlement was at a site called the "clearing beyond the rapids," whose "fortress" section used a defense system called the zaseka, and some settlers mixed with the Nogay people on the Terek River
examples: The "Eyewitness Chronicle" is a 18th-century manuscript history of these people, whose first major settlement was at a site called the "clearing beyond the rapids."

clues.clue: British historian Shane O'Rourke has argued that certain twentieth-century ethnic-assimilation measures directed against them could constitute genocide
examples: The British historian Shane O'Rourke, whose two books are about the history of these people, has argued that certain ethnic assimilation measures directed at them could constitute genocide.

works.name: The Zaporozhian Sich and the Cossack Hetmanate
works.description: Their first major settlement and proto-state was founded on the island of Khortytsia (now a national park and museum), giving rise to the Zaporozhian Sich or Host, a self-governing polity headquartered in the region of Zaporizhzhia ("land beyond the rapids") and led by chief officers called koshovyi. A demand to increase the number of "registered" Cossacks recognized by the Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth helped spark the Czaplinski Affair and the 1648 Khmelnytsky Uprising, led by hetman Bohdan Khmelnytsky, during which they sought a "three-nation" Commonwealth but instead pledged allegiance to Tsar Alexis of Russia at the Pereyaslav Council; the unamended Treaty of Hadiach would separately have elevated some of them to the nobility. A later hetman, Pylyp Orlyk, drafted a 1710 constitution at Bendery -- guaranteed by the exiled Swedish king Charles XII -- that created a legislative General Council and anticipated Montesquieu's separation of powers, but a previous hetman, Ivan Mazepa, had already betrayed Peter the Great for Charles XII at the 1709 Battle of Poltava.

clues.clue: Their first proto-state/major settlement was on the island of Khortytsia (now a national park and museum), giving its name to the Zaporozhian Sich/Host, based in the region of Zaporizhzhia ("land beyond the rapids") and governed by chief officers called koshovyi
examples: The national park on the island of Khortytsia includes a museum dedicated to these people in the place where they established their first proto-state.
Identify this region whose namesake Host was governed by chief officers called the koshovyi ("kuh-SHOHV-yee").

Ivan Mazepa and Bogdan Khmelnytsky were among the hetmans of the Zaporozhian branch of this ethnicity.

clues.clue: Pylyp Orlyk was one of these people, as was Baida, the founder of one of their subgroups

works.name: Depictions in Russian Art: Ilya Repin's "Reply of the Zaporozhian Cossacks"
works.description: Their most famous artistic depiction is Ilya Repin's 1891 painting showing them laughing uproariously as they jointly compose a mocking written reply to Ottoman Sultan Mehmed IV's demand for surrender -- legendarily telling him he "could not slay a hedgehog with his naked arse" or calling him "the crick in [their] dick," with insults such as "Goat-fucker of Alexandria"; the painting shows a fat man in a red suit and white fur hat and another member in a pink jacket. For that painting, the Grand Chamberlain of a court refused to pose, so Repin secretly sketched the back of his bald head, and a gypsum mask was used to capture the likeness of an ill former student; an opera singer, painters, and a strongman also served as models. Repin painted them elsewhere too, packed into a boat called a chaika during a storm in Cossacks on the Black Sea and, late in life, an elaborately dressed member of the group in The Hopak; other artists depicted them as well, including Bogdan Willewalde's painting of them greeting children in Bautzen and the painting They Were Imprisoned, showing three dejected men in red-plumed helmets being captured by them.

clues.clue: Ilya Repin's 1891 painting shows them laughing while jointly composing a mocking, insult-filled written reply refusing Ottoman Sultan Mehmed IV's demand for surrender
examples: Ilya Repin painted a group of what people "replying" to Mehmed IV?
Mehmed IV was told that he "could not slay a hedgehog with his naked arse" in a reply by the Zaporozhian group of these people, a scene painted by Ilya Repin.

clues.clue: For that painting, the Grand Chamberlain of a court refused to pose, so the artist secretly sketched the back of his bald head, and a gypsum mask was used to capture the likeness of an ill former student; an opera singer, painters, and a strongman also served as models
examples: The Grand Chamberlain of a court refused to pose for a painting of these people, leading its artist to clandestinely sketch the back of his bald head.

clues.clue: Repin also painted them packed into a boat called a chaika during a storm in Cossacks on the Black Sea, and late in life painted an elaborately dressed member of the group in The Hopak
examples: A group of these people appear packed into a chaika during a storm in a painting titled for them "on the Black Sea."
Just before dying, Repin painted an elaborately dressed member of this group in The Hopak.

examples: A group of these people greet children in Bautzen in a Bogdan Willewalde painting.
The painting They Were Imprisoned depicts three dejected men with red plumed helmets being captured by these people.

works.name: Literary Depictions
works.description: They are central to several major works of Russian literature. Leo Tolstoy's novella The Cossacks follows the Russian protagonist Olenin, who fights in the Caucasian War, befriends the old hunter Eroshka, and pursues a romance with Maryanka -- his marriage plans are complicated when a rival, Luka, is shot in the belly during a raid. Mikhail Sholokhov's novel And Quiet Flows the Don follows a member of their community who saves a boy from drowning and impresses others by shooting pheasants on the wing, and who falls in love with Aksinia but whips her upon learning of her affair with Eugene. In Nikolai Gogol's novella Taras Bulba, the title character, one of their number, is tied to a tree and burned alive at the story's end, and Anna Akhmatova's poem Requiem includes the line "silent flows the river Don," possibly referencing their folk songs.

clues.clue: Leo Tolstoy's novella The Cossacks follows the protagonist Olenin, who fights in the Caucasian War, befriends the old hunter Eroshka, and pursues a romance with Maryanka; his marriage plans are complicated when a rival, Luka, is shot in the belly during a raid
examples: That character befriends Eroshka in a novel titled for these people in which they clash with a group of Chechens.
The protagonist Olenin attempts to marry a member of this group in a Tolstoy novel titled for them.

clues.clue: Mikhail Sholokhov's novel And Quiet Flows the Don follows a member of their community who saves a boy from drowning, impresses others by shooting pheasants on the wing, and falls in love with Aksinia but whips her upon learning of her affair with Eugene
examples: In another novel, one of these people falls in love with Aksinia but whips her when he finds out she has been having an affair with Eugene.

examples: Requiem's line "silent flows the river Don" may be a reference to the folk songs of these people.

works.name: World War II and the Repatriation Question
works.description: During World War II, many defected from the Soviet Union to serve in German cavalry units, and the German officer Helmuth von Pannwitz organized a volunteer Cossack cavalry force that was incorporated into the Waffen-SS against his will in February 1945. Their postwar fate is often called a "betrayal": under Operation Keelhaul, as many as a million prisoners of war and Nazi collaborators (Cossacks and others) were forcibly repatriated to the Soviet Union, with many executed at Judenberg after the British handed them over per terms agreed at the Yalta Conference, where Churchill had agreed to oversee such repatriations.

clues.clue: Many defected from the Soviet Union to serve in German cavalry units in World War II, and Helmuth von Pannwitz organized a volunteer Cossack cavalry force incorporated into the Waffen-SS against his will in February 1945
examples: Many of them defected from the Soviet Union to serve in German cavalry units during World War II.
Helmuth von Pannwitz established a volunteer force of these people who were incorporated into the Waffen SS against his will in February 1945.

clues.clue: Under Operation Keelhaul, often called their "betrayal," many were forcibly repatriated to the Soviet Union and executed at Judenberg after the British handed them over per terms agreed at the Yalta Conference
examples: In an event called the "betrayal" of these people during Operation Keelhaul, a number of POWs who were these people were executed in Judenberg after the British turned them over in accordance with terms agreed to at Yalta.

works.name: The Russian Civil War and Decossackization
works.description: After the Russian Civil War, Soviet officials in their khutor and stanitsa communities were given orders to extirpate them as an independent group, a policy known as decossackization; their Zaporozhian and Kuban branches were specifically targeted for extermination because they had sided with the White Army against the Bolsheviks.

clues.clue: After the Russian Civil War, officials in their khutor and stanitsa communities were ordered to extirpate them as an independent group; their Zaporozhian and Kuban branches were targeted for extermination for having sided with the White Army
examples: The executives of the khutor and stanitsa communities in which most of these people lived were given orders to extirpate them as an independent group during the Russian Civil War.
These people were targeted for extermination after the Russian Civil War, since their Zaporozhian and Kuban groups had sided with the White Army.

works.name: Revolts Against the Tsars
works.description: They repeatedly rose up against the Russian tsars. The Yaik Host, whose members were often recruited into the Russian Army, revolted under Yemelyan Pugachev, who took Kazan from Catherine the Great during his rebellion. Earlier, Stenka Razin led an uprising during the reign of Tsar Alexius, drawing on Cossacks who lived near the Don River, were led by hetmen, and prized horses. Their poor relationship with Peter the Great -- many of them living along the Don River -- also produced the Bulavin Rebellion, which he suppressed by killing many of them.

clues.clue: The Yaik Host, often recruited into the Russian Army, revolted under Yemelyan Pugachev, who took Kazan from Catherine the Great during his rebellion
examples: The city of Kazan was taken from Catherine the Great during a revolt by which group of Slavic raiders that included Yemelyan Pugachev?
Name these people often recruited into the Russian Army whose Yaik host revolted under Yemelyan Pugachev.

clues.clue: Stenka Razin, one of their number, led an uprising during the reign of Tsar Alexius; many lived near the Don River, were led by hetmen, and prized horses
examples: Stenka Razin, a member of this ethnic group, led an uprising during Alexius's reign. Many of these people lived near the Don River, they were led by hetmen and they liked horses a lot.

clues.clue: Their poor relationship with Peter the Great -- many living along the Don River -- produced the Bulavin Rebellion, which he suppressed by killing many of them
examples: Peter the Great had such a poor relationship with these independent-minded people, some of whom lived along the Don River, that they rose up in the Bulavin Rebellion.

works.name: Beyond Russia: The Persian Cossack Brigade and Cultural Legacy
works.description: Their model spread beyond Russia: an elite brigade of the late Qajar dynasty in Iran was named for and modeled on them, and one of its officers, Reza Khan, mobilized the brigade for the 1921 coup that overthrew the Qajars. In music, the Czech composer Antonin Dvorak incorporated the folk-song form called the dumka -- popularized by the composer Mykola Lysenko, who was descended from them -- into his chamber works, while Franz Liszt turned a piano etude named for one of their leaders, Mazeppa, into a symphonic tone poem with sections named for his "ride" and "march." In New Zealand, Prime Minister Robert Muldoon aired a television commercial featuring historical figures among them, including Ivan Mazeppa and Yemelyan Pugachev, dancing across the screen to warn that the Labour government was communist.

clues.clue: An elite brigade of the late Qajar dynasty in Iran was named for and modeled on them; officer Reza Khan mobilized the brigade for the 1921 coup that overthrew the Qajars
examples: Name these soldiers who comprised an elite brigade of the late Qajar dynasty. One of their officers, Reza Khan, mobilized them for the 1921 coup that overthrew the Qajars.

clues.clue: Dvorak incorporated the dumka, popularized by the composer Mykola Lysenko (descended from them), into his chamber works; Liszt turned a piano etude named for their leader Mazeppa into a tone poem with "ride" and "march" sections
examples: Dvorak incorporated the dumka into his piano trio and quintet after it was popularized by Mykola Lysenko, a composer descended from these people. Another composer turned a piano etude named for a leader of these people into a tone poem with sections named for his "ride" and "march."

clues.clue: New Zealand PM Robert Muldoon aired a TV commercial featuring historical figures among them, including Ivan Mazeppa and Yemelyan Pugachev, dancing to warn that the Labour government was communist
examples: Muldoon also created a hilarious TV commercial in which several of these historical figures dance across the screen and tell the audience that the Labour government is communist.

comprehensive_summary: The Cossacks are a militaristic East Slavic people of the Eastern European steppe frontier, especially along the Don and Dnieper Rivers, who never ate bread according to one papal diplomat and supposedly got their name from a term for the comparative agility of goats. Organized through community councils called rada that elected chieftains called hetmans and other officers called atamans, with a gem-encrusted mace called the bulawa as their symbol of power, their history is recorded in an 18th-century manuscript called the Eyewitness Chronicle. Their first proto-state was founded on the island of Khortytsia, giving rise to the Zaporozhian Sich in the region of Zaporizhzhia ("land beyond the rapids"), governed by koshovyi officers. A demand to increase their "registered" numbers helped spark the Czaplinski Affair and the 1648 Khmelnytsky Uprising, led by hetman Bohdan Khmelnytsky, during which they sought a "three-nation" Commonwealth but instead pledged allegiance to Tsar Alexis of Russia at the Pereyaslav Council (the unamended Treaty of Hadiach would separately have ennobled some of them). Hetman Ivan Mazepa later betrayed Peter the Great for Swedish king Charles XII at the 1709 Battle of Poltava, after which hetman Pylyp Orlyk drafted a 1710 constitution at Bendery, guaranteed by the exiled Charles XII, that anticipated Montesquieu's separation of powers. They repeatedly revolted against the tsars: Stenka Razin under Alexius, the Bulavin Rebellion against Peter the Great, and Yemelyan Pugachev's Yaik Host uprising, during which Kazan was seized from Catherine the Great. Their most famous artistic depiction is Ilya Repin's 1891 painting showing them laughing as they compose an insult-filled reply refusing Ottoman Sultan Mehmed IV's demand for surrender ("could not slay a hedgehog with his naked arse"), for which a Grand Chamberlain refused to pose and a gypsum mask stood in for an ill model; Repin also painted them in Cossacks on the Black Sea and, late in life, The Hopak, while other artists like Bogdan Willewalde depicted them as well. They loom large in Russian literature: Leo Tolstoy's novella The Cossacks (Olenin, Eroshka, Maryanka), Mikhail Sholokhov's And Quiet Flows the Don (Aksinia and Eugene), Nikolai Gogol's Taras Bulba (the title character burned alive), and a line in Anna Akhmatova's Requiem. In the twentieth century, many defected from the Soviet Union to fight for Germany in World War II, including in Helmuth von Pannwitz's Waffen-SS cavalry force, and were subject afterward to the mass forced repatriation of Operation Keelhaul following the Yalta Conference; after the Russian Civil War, Soviet decossackization policy ordered their khutor and stanitsa communities extirpated for having sided with the White Army. Their model and legacy spread beyond Russia, from Iran's Persian Cossack Brigade (used by Reza Khan in the 1921 coup) to Dvorak's and Liszt's music and even a satirical Cold War-era New Zealand political ad.
recursive_suggestions: The Reply of the Zaporozhian Cossacks to Sultan Mehmed IV of the Ottoman Empire -- appeared as its own dense Fine Arts answerline (models refusing to pose, a smaller pipe-less version, record sale price to Alexander III) independent of the Cossacks-as-people clues; excluded here per the different-actual-answer rule and worth a standalone Visual Fine Arts page (Ilya Repin already has a page).

Forced repatriations to the Soviet Union after World War II (Operation Keelhaul) -- appeared as its own dense History answerline (Lord Aldington libel suit, Nikolai Tolstoy's The Minister and the Massacres, Bleiburg) independent of the Cossacks themselves; excluded here and worth a standalone topic.
Bohdan Khmelnytsky -- recurring hetman/uprising-leader figure without an existing page; potential standalone History topic.

Roman von Ungern-Sternberg (the "Mad Baron") -- a Cossack regiment officer with independent biographical clue content (Asiatic Cavalry Division, 1921 restoration of a Buddhist monarchy in Mongolia) surfaced in this search; excluded as a different actual answer and worth a standalone figure page.
Ambiguity flag: the unrelated "Cossacks Motorcycle Club," a Waco, Texas biker gang involved in a 2015 shootout, surfaced as a same-named but wholly unrelated answerline and was excluded entirely.
